title: Repetitive Strain and Cumulative Trauma Disorder
synonyms: Repetitive Strain and Cumulative Trauma Disorder

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Repetitive strain and cumulative trauma presentations arise when repeated low-level load, posture, force, or vibration exceeds tissue recovery over time.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Gradual onset pain, stiffness, or paraesthesia
- Symptoms linked to repetitive typing, gripping, scanning, drilling, or tool use
- Worse late in shift or after consecutive workdays
- Reduced tolerance rather than one clear injury event

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Distinguish overload syndromes from inflammatory disease, peripheral nerve entrapment, or acute trauma
- Repetition alone is not the only driver; task pace, force, posture, vibration, and recovery matter
- Workers may underreport early symptoms until capacity is substantially reduced

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Identify the repetitive task pattern, symptom trend, and whether immediate task modification is required
- Provide early activity modification advice and avoid pushing through escalating symptoms
- Escalate if weakness, marked numbness, or severe swelling suggests a more serious process

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Analyse exposure volume, force, posture, breaks, and equipment
- Tailor load reduction, strengthening, mobility, pacing, and ergonomic changes to the specific task
- Review work organisation factors such as overtime, role rotation, and production pressure
- Refer for medical review or specialist opinion if neurological findings, persistent severe pain, or inflammatory features are present

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Cumulative trauma management usually fails if the worker returns to the same exposure pattern unchanged
